schools.summary: Gill: these are not the ten commandments repeated, but all others, moral, ceremonial, and judicial, a fresh declaration of the laws, given to teach the people that they might do them in the land.

themes: these are the commandments... which the LORD your God commanded to teach you, that ye might do them
the teaching aimed at obedience
God's law given to be lived

schools.summary: Gill: the fear meant is not slavish but filial, a reverential affection for God, as of children to their Father.
Keil & Delitzsch: the keeping of his statutes all the days of life.

themes: That thou mightest fear the LORD thy God, to keep all his statutes... all the days of thy life
a filial fear, not slavish dread
the root of obedience (Prov. 1:7)

schools.summary: Gill: the charge to hear and observe to do the commandments given them.
Keil & Delitzsch: the fear of God would bring well-being and mighty increase in the land of milk and honey.

themes: Hear therefore, O Israel, and observe to do it; that it may be well with thee
hearing made good in doing
obedience the way of welfare and blessing

schools.summary: Gill: the words of Moses stirring the people to attend to this great article, the unity of God, to prevent polytheism and idolatry; from this the Jews call the section the Shema, read twice daily.

themes: Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God is one LORD
the foundation-stone of true religion
the first commandment, owned by Christ (Mark 12:29)

schools.summary: Gill: this is the first and chief commandment, the sum of the first table; it includes knowledge of God, esteem, delight, faith, fear, worship, and obedience, which when right springs from it; God is to be loved for the perfections of his being.

themes: thou shalt love the LORD thy God with all thine heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy might
the first and great commandment
love the root of all acceptable service (Matt. 22:37)

schools.summary: Gill: they were to take notice that God is one and to be loved in the strongest manner.
Keil & Delitzsch: for the love of God to be right, his commandments must be laid to heart.

themes: these words, which I command thee this day, shall be in thine heart
the seat of religion within
the law written in the heart (Jer. 31:33)

schools.summary: Gill: care and pains are to be used to instruct children early; the word may be rendered 'thou shalt whet or sharpen them,' to impress them keenly; the words to be talked of at home and abroad, lying down and rising up.

themes: thou shalt teach them diligently unto thy children, and shalt talk of them when thou sittest
religion filling the whole of life
the faith handed from age to age (Eph. 6:4)

schools.summary: Gill: as a man ties anything to his hand for a token to remember it, so were these words to be kept in mind; the Jews understand it literally of phylacteries.
Clarke: treats the binding upon the hand.

themes: thou shalt bind them for a sign upon thine hand, and they shall be as frontlets between thine eyes
the constant remembrance of God's law
the life governed by his word

schools.summary: Gill: this was to put them in mind when they went out and came in; the Jews take it literally, writing the section on a scroll (mezuzah) and fixing it on the posts.

themes: thou shalt write them upon the posts of thy house, and on thy gates
God's word in the path of daily life
the home marked for God and his word

schools.summary: Gill: the land of Canaan, with its great cities. Calvin: since wealth and prosperity for the most part blind men's minds, so that they grow wanton and forget moderation, Moses warns them beforehand.

themes: when the LORD thy God shall have brought thee into the land... great and goodly cities, which thou buildedst not
prosperity a subtle snare
the heart guarded most when things are full

schools.summary: Gill: houses full of good furniture and the fruits of the earth, corn, wine, oil, perhaps gold and silver.
Keil & Delitzsch: the wells, vineyards, and olive trees they had not made.

themes: houses full of all good things, which thou filledst not... when thou shalt have eaten and be full
God's lavish goodness a test of the heart
every good gift a call to thankfulness (James 1:17)

schools.summary: Gill: creature enjoyments are apt to get possession of the heart and its affections, so beware of forgetting God.
Clarke: in earthly prosperity men are apt to forget heavenly things, while the senses have all they wish.

themes: beware lest thou forget the LORD, which brought thee forth out of the land of Egypt
the great peril of plenty
a heart holding fast its Redeemer

schools.summary: Gill: the fear is filial, a reverential affection for the God who brought them from bondage into liberty and plenty; they should fear and serve him.
Clarke: one of the passages Christ used to repel the temptations of Satan (Matt. 4:10).

themes: Thou shalt fear the LORD thy God, and serve him, and shalt swear by his name
the whole duty toward God
Christ's answer to Satan, him only shalt thou serve (Matt. 4:10)

schools.summary: Gill: to go after other gods is to serve and worship them, and depart from the true God; these are no gods, only nominal and fictitious, idols that are nothing in the world.
Clarke: warns against following other gods.

themes: Ye shall not go after other gods, of the gods of the people which are round about you
the idols nothing in the world
the love of the one LORD admitting no rival

schools.summary: Gill: God was near them, his tabernacle between their camps; a God jealous of his honour in worship, who would resent any affront.
Keil & Delitzsch: the warning lest his anger be kindled.

themes: For the LORD thy God is a jealous God among you
God's holy zeal claiming the whole heart
idolatry a spiritual adultery (Exod. 34:14)

schools.summary: Gill: to tempt God is to strive against him, murmur at his providences, or require a sign; this is another passage Christ used to repel Satan.
Clarke: they were not to provoke him by entertaining doubts of his presence or care.

themes: Ye shall not tempt the LORD your God, as ye tempted him in Massah
the unbelief that demands fresh signs
Christ's answer to Satan (Matt. 4:7)

schools.summary: Gill: not only the ten commands but all others were to be kept.
Henry: it requires a great deal of care and pains to keep up religion in the power of it in our hearts and lives, negligence being its undoing.

themes: Ye shall diligently keep the commandments of the LORD your God
religion's power requiring care and pains
love proved in earnest, watchful obedience

schools.summary: Gill: what is right and good appears from the declaration of God's will in his commandments, which are holy, just, and good; obeying them is doing right, that it may be well.

themes: thou shalt do that which is right and good in the sight of the LORD: that it may be well with thee
God's will the standard of right
obedience the path of blessing (Rom. 7:12)

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD promised, and as it seems with an oath, to drive out their enemies and make way for their settlement in the land.

themes: To cast out all thine enemies from before thee, as the LORD hath spoken
the conquest God's own work
the inheritance secured by his faithfulness

schools.summary: Gill: 'in time to come,' or 'tomorrow,' meaning in later times, after they came into Canaan and the ordinances took effect.
Clarke, with Ainsworth: marks the place of catechizing children in the faith.

themes: when thy son asketh thee in time to come... What mean the testimonies
the faith handed on through children's questions
taught with its reasons, not by rote

schools.summary: Gill: the answer leads the son into the spring and original of the commandments, and acquaints him with the goodness of God that laid them under obligation to obey.
Keil & Delitzsch: we were Pharaoh's bondmen, and the LORD brought us out with a mighty hand.

themes: We were Pharaoh's bondmen in Egypt; and the LORD brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand
the law grounded in redemption
obedience the grateful response of the redeemed (1 John 4:19)

schools.summary: Gill: the ten plagues, signs of the power of God, great and sore, very distressing to the Egyptians, came heavy upon Pharaoh and all his household.

themes: the LORD shewed signs and wonders, great and sore, upon Egypt... before our eyes
the wonders of the redemption rehearsed
the children knowing the greatness of their Redeemer

schools.summary: Gill: he brought them out of a state of bondage and misery, in order to bring them into the land sworn to their fathers.

themes: he brought us out from thence, that he might bring us in... the land which he sware unto our fathers
redemption with a purpose
he who begins the work completes it (Phil. 1:6)

schools.summary: Gill: some statutes were designed to commemorate the deliverance, as the passover; all were to be obeyed in gratitude, commanded for their good always, that God might preserve them.

themes: the LORD commanded us to do all these statutes... for our good always, that he might preserve us alive
the law a gift of love
obedience the way of life (Deut. 10:13)

schools.summary: Gill: to have such a justifying righteousness a man must keep all the commandments of God, not one excepted, and that perfectly, without the least failure.

themes: it shall be our righteousness, if we observe to do all these commandments
perfect obedience no fallen man can render
the law a schoolmaster to Christ (Gal. 3:24)
